title: Lesson 435
transcript: Spare parts. We're gonna spend a few minutes here talking about some different options that you can have for having spare parts. So to put this in context, what this is about is having spare parts available such that if a part fails in a system, you've got a spare to replace it with. And we're going to talk through some different options here, cold, warm, and hot spares, and what the differences are and what these provide. But to put this into context, imagine a server and the spare part that we're talking about is the power supply. So if the primary power supply fails, where is our spare part? And really what the differences break down to between all of these is that it's two things that we're gonna look at for each of them. If the primary part fails, is the system going down? So that's the first thing. If the primary part fails, is the system going down? And then the second difference we'll look at between these is how long does it take the system to get back up and running? So is the system going down and how long does it take to get back up and running? Let's now start with a cold spare. What a cold spare means is that it's a spare part on a shelf somewhere, maybe not even in the same building. That's what a cold spare means. Spare part on a shelf somewhere, maybe not even in the same building. So using the power supply as an example, if the only power supply in the system fails, is the system going down? Absolutely, right? Because the spare part is nowhere near. So the system is absolutely going down. How long is it going to take to recover the system? I mean, if you ever replaced a power supply, it takes a while. You've got to pull all the cabling out of the case, put everything back in, and it probably takes half an hour to replace a power supply. And if the power supply is in another building somewhere and you've got to get it shipped in, it could be anywhere from a few minutes to a few hours to maybe in a few days. The point is it's going to take a while. to get the system back up and running with a cold spare. So this is obviously the cheapest solution, but it has some pretty significant downsides. Now, listen really carefully here to the definition of a warm spare. It's subtle. A warm spare is a spare part installed in the system, but not, not powered on and ready to go. So a warm spare is a spare part installed in the system, but it's not, not powered on and ready to go. So if you're using a warm spare power supply and the primary fails, is the system going down? Yes, because the warm spare is not powered on. It's not ready to go. So yes, the system is going down. What's the recovery time? Maybe just a few seconds, maybe just a few minutes. Someone has to run over to the server, manually switch over to the alternate power supply, and then power the system back on. So that's a warm spare. Spare part installed in the system, but not powered on and ready to go. So the system is going down, but it's only going to take a few seconds or maybe a few minutes to bring back up. And you can probably guess what the definition is now of a hot spare. A hot spare is a spare part installed in the system and it is, it is powered on and ready to go. And so with a hot spare, if the primary power supply fails, is the system going down? No, because if you're using a hot spare setup, the server will have been designed to have essentially two power supplies in it that are powered on, ready to go at all times. There'll be the primary and say the secondary. And the server will normally be operating off the primary power supply. But if it were to ever fail, the system would just automatically fail over to the hot spare secondary power supply and the system would not go down. So obviously you don't have to think about what the recovery time would be. So that leads to a good question. Which of these is best? And the answer is, it depends. What does it depend on? The perfect number that it depends on is the RTO, the recovery time objective. How quickly do you need to bring the system back up? That's really what determines which of these is best. If a system owner says to you, you know what, it's fine if this system is down for like two days, just make sure it's cheap, cold spare, right? At the other extreme, if the owner of the system says, this system can never be down and I'm willing to pay a lot of money to make that happen, then a hot spare would be the best option. So that's why we have these different options. You're going to pick the one that best suits the needs of the owner of that system, best suits the needs of the organization. There is no best. Depends.
